text: The surgeon performs a perineal staple rectal resection to address a full-thickness, large rectal prolapse in a frail elderly patient.

Here we present a ninety year old female patient with a past medical history of atrial fibrillation, history of primary excision for squamous cell carcinoma from the lower leg in two thousand seventeen, local recurrence exercise in two thousand eighteen and further lymphadenectomy for recurrence in the left groin in two thousand eighteen.
The patient presented with a full thickness rectal prolapse measuring ten centimeters which was having a significant negative impact on her quality of life.
She was offered a perineal stapled prolapse resection, which is a relatively novel surgical procedure for treating external rectal prolapse with good clinical outcome.

Frail patients who are unsuitable for prolonged abdominal procedures and can only endure a short procedure under regional anesthesia should be considered for perineal stapled rectal prolapse resection.
PSPR is also beneficial for elderly patients with a large rectal prolapse, approximately eight to ten centimeters, and multiple comorbidities.
In addition, PSPR has shown equally good clinical outcomes with young patients who want to avoid potential complication of nerve damage that is associated with the abdominal approach.
The patient is positioned in the lithotomy position with a slight Trendelenburg tilt, preparing for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon reproduces the rectal prolapse by grasping the apex of the prolapsed rectal tissue.
The surgeon resects the prolapsed rectum longitudinally at the three o'clock position using a reloadable 100mm GIA linear stapler, visibly excising the prolapsed tissue.
The surgeon waits for at least one minute before firing the stapler, allowing for the thickness and edema of the rectal tissues to settle before proceeding with the stapling.
The surgeon repeats the resection of the rectal prolapse at the nine o'clock position using a reloadable 100mm GIA linear stapler.
The surgeon has created anterior and posterior prolapsed rectal flaps, visible as distinct tissue sections during the procedure.
The surgeon resects the anterior rectal flap horizontally at the anorectal junction, carefully completing the resection parallel to and avoiding the dentate line.
The surgeon uses a reloadable endo-GIA linear stapling device, which is flexible and can be angled, to resect the posterior prolapsed rectal flap.
The surgeon uses a contour stapler for precise transection, resulting in the anal mucosa and neorectum spontaneously falling back into place after the complete resection.
